I spent 6 months learning React. Then I built the same feature in HTMX in 20 minutes. Here’s what I learned.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Hook: My “Wait, What?” Moment
I thought I had it figured out. React. Hooks. State management. Build tools. I was that guy who could spin up a Vite project in 30 seconds and explain why useEffect cleanup functions matter.
Then my backend teammate — a Python developer who thinks JavaScript is a punishment — built a feature faster than me.
Not just faster. Way faster. He didn’t touch React. He didn’t install npm. He didn’t create a node_modules folder that could store the entire internet.
He wrote standard HTML with a few extra attributes. And it worked. It was interactive. It fetched data. It updated the page. And the entire thing was 14 kilobytes.
I felt something I hadn’t felt since bootcamp: confusion.
What is this sorcery? Why does it feel like cheating? And why am I suddenly questioning every life choice that led me to npm install?
That sorcery is called HTMX. And it’s not a JavaScript framework. It’s not even a framework at all.
